learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of curriculum content and critically analyse relevant Science concepts, topics and effective teaching strategies used to teach in this curriculum learning area.

Apply curriculum, assessment and reporting knowledge to design appropriate Science learning experiences, set appropriate learning goals for varying abilities, organise content into effective learning and teaching sequences, and select appropriate resources, including ICT with consideration for safe, responsible and ethical use, to support and engage student learning.

Apply research informed knowledge of student learning, knowledge of Science education and relevant curriculum documents and other sources of professional learning informed by APST to critically analyse effective planning for learning, teaching strategies, and evaluate teaching programs.

Demonstrate informed knowledge of teaching strategies that support inclusive participation and engagement in Science learning activities. These include effective verbal and non-verbal communication strategies and strategies to support student safety.
Demonstrate understanding of different assessment approaches such as diagnostic, informal/formal, formative and summative assessment that assess Science learning.
Demonstrate clear, fluent and coherent communication skills consistent with personal, professional and academic conventions.

Work effectively and collaboratively in teams to make informed team decisions, demonstrate interpersonal, leadership and communication skills, and critically evaluate personal and peer contributions to team processes.

assessments.task_description:
All students are expected to complete a safety declaration in order to work in the laboratory. The university requires this declaration to be current within 5 years. Records are kept on the OHS database. Laboratory conditions for education are considered to be any space where scientific activity is taking place. Students must be aware that this also translates to primary classrooms where safety is paramount, and risk assessment is critical.

APST: 4.4
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
From Cookbook to Inquiry

In this assessment task, you will design an investigation beginning with an experiment that involves the collection of numerical data and demonstrates how that can contribute to a scientific explanation and lead to an in-class investigation. You need to include:

  • An introduction that demonstrates your knowledge and understanding of the research into how students learn Science, the importance of experiments in student learning in the Science classroom, the experiment itself and its place in the curriculum. Your introduction should contain enough information and critical detail for a colleague to understand the implications for teaching.
  • A detailed annotated procedure for students to carry out the experiment. The annotation should provide information that another teacher can use to implement the experiment successfully. Include a Risk Assessment to identify strategies you would use to ensure student safety.
  • You will need to perform the experiment yourself. Include the set of results you recorded and an explanation of the Science that underpins the experiment. Discuss the strategies you would use to ensure students develop appropriate understandings of the science concept.
  • Consider how you could challenge students to use their experience with the experiment to identify and investigate a different but related question. In a lesson plan, indicate how you structure the classroom environment to support inclusive participation and engagement in the investigation.
  • Justify the position of this task within the curriculum with reasoning for its inclusion in an assessment profile for reporting.

APST: 1.2, 2.1, 2.2, 3.1, 3.2, 3.3, 4.1, 4.4


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Evaluating an Inquiry Unit of Work
assessments.task_description:
A unit plan is necessary to organise content into an effective learning and teaching sequence over a period of time, e.g., a term. In Primary Science, the resources we select should support this coherence by meaningfully supporting student engagement and understanding.

In this task, you will analyse an existing unit of work using a CoRe Matrix and evaluate it against the Australian Curriculum: Science V9 through the lens of Constructive Alignment, which focuses on the coherence between learning outcomes, teaching strategies, and assessment.

The task consists of two parts.

 Part 1 – Analysis (approx. 1000 words)

Critique a Primary Connections unit of work and use this analysis to develop a CoRe Matrix for the three phases of the unit (Launch, Inquire, Act). The CoRe matrix provides a holistic representation of a sequential unit of work. It helps identify students’ prior knowledge, possible misconceptions, and effective teaching strategies and assessment approaches that promote student learning. [The Matrix template will be provided and modelled in class.]

Building on your critique and through your CoRe matrix, demonstrate a clear understanding of the unit’s conceptual focus and pedagogical intent. This includes articulating an Essential Question for the unit and outlining the background knowledge necessary for effective teaching. Any text taken directly from the Primary Connections resource must be in italics and appropriately referenced.       

Part 2 – Critique (approx. 1000 words)

In this section, you will extend your analysis by evaluating the unit through the lens of Constructive Alignment. Your task is to consider both how well the unit aligns learning outcomes, teaching strategies, and assessment, and why this alignment is essential for designing meaningful Primary Science learning.

Your discussion should focus on the significance of alignment for coherent planning: how clear intentions guide purposeful teaching, how aligned learning experiences support conceptual development, and how assessment provides meaningful evidence of learning. You should also reflect on what engaging with Constructive Alignment reveals about your own developing practice and how it shapes your approach to designing intentional, responsive science learning. 

APST: 2.2, 2.3, 3.1, 3.2, 3.4, 3.6, 5.1


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please use the Turnitin Submission link on Blackboard to upload your task as one combined Word document (CoRe Matrix table and reflection). Ideally, 11pt font with 1.5 line spacing.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Working in groups of three (3), you will select a science concept to teach to your peers. You will collaboratively design and deliver a 20-minute LIA (Launch-Inquire-Act) micro-teaching episode that teaches a science concept based on a content description from the Australian Curriculum: Science. Your presentation should model inquiry-based science teaching and demonstrate how the phenomenon can be used as an anchor for conceptual development. Your presentation must:

  • Introduce the lesson segment by briefly explaining where your phase fits within the full LIA sequence and how the task(s) in your segment backmap to the intended summative assessment.
  • Teach your allocated phase of the lesson, demonstrating clear pedagogical intent, accurate science content, and strategies that support inclusive participation and engagement.
  • Use a range of verbal and non‑verbal communication strategies to enhance clarity, support inquiry, and promote student understanding.
  • Address safety and classroom environment considerations, including any required risk assessments relevant to your phase.
  • Respond to questions without notice from peers and your tutor, demonstrating your understanding of the science concept, the inquiry process, and your pedagogical choices.

The focus of this task is on developing Pedagogical Content Knowledge, modelling inquiry through the LIA structure, and demonstrating how direct teaching can be used purposefully within an inquiry‑based approach.

The micro-teaching episode will be video-recorded for your reflection (Assessment task 4b).

All group members must contribute equally – both in terms of planning and organisation as well as to the presentation itself, i.e., each person presents for the same amount of time.

APST: 1.2, 2.2, 3.1, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 6.2


A note about group work:

You will receive a single group mark for this task, as all group members should contribute equally to the design and delivery of the assessment task. However, exceptions can be made in the event of one or more group members engaging in ‘social loafing’ (also known as ‘free-riding’):

Free riding is a form of social loafing seen in a group when one or more members slack off and ‘ride’ on the extra efforts of their co-workers (Walker & Angelo, 1998).

If your small group is experiencing dysfunction, including ‘social loafing’ by one or more members, it is your responsibility to bring this promptly to the attention of your tutor / course coordinator, who will then support the group to resolve the problems in a respectful way. If no resolution can be found, the tutor / course coordinator can take steps, including the removal of the non-contributing student/s from the group and requiring the student/s to complete the task either individually or in another small group with other ‘social loafers’.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Presentation will occur during class time in Week 13.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of assessment without approved extension request via myUQ system will result in a failing grade.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Teaching a Science Concept: Part 2 (Mainstream Program)
assessments.task_description:
After your teaching event, you will receive specific feedback from several of your peers. You will also have access to the video recording of your presentation.

Write an individual critical reflection on your mini‑lesson, supported by relevant literature. Your reflection should include:

  1. a brief lesson outline for your micro-teaching episode, including links to the Australian Curriculum: Science, appropriate goals, resources (including one online resource) and background notes (brief) (approx. 600 words; group development);
  2. a personal critical review of the mini-lesson acknowledging the contribution of your peers to your learning and arguing the strengths and tensions of inquiry-based learning (approx. 500 words; individual);
  3. consideration for strategies you will use to ensure students engage with your identified online resource in a safe, responsible and ethical way (approx. 400 words; individual);
  4. a statement of your science teaching philosophy: What are your beliefs and values for this curriculum area? How could they apply to students, their parents/carers and the wider community? (approx. 500 words; individual)
  5. a reflection on professional learning: What kind of ongoing professional learning will you need to improve student learning and your teaching of Science? Can you identify various relevant and appropriate sources of professional learning and articulate their relevance for your future teaching practice? Draw on the Australian Professional Standards for Teachers to identify particular professional learning needs to improve your practice. (approx. 500 words; individual)

APST: 1.2, 2.2, 3.1, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 4.5, 6.1, 6.2


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. learning_activities.topic:
Module 1: Teaching Science

The introductory module will provide an overview of the importance and challenges of teaching science to primary-age students in an increasingly STEM-oriented world. Models of curriculum and pedagogy will be explored in advance of first-hand experience in schools.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 1: Teaching Science The introductory module will provide an overview of the importance and challenges of teaching science to primary-age students in an increasingly STEM-oriented world. Models of curriculum and pedagogy will be explored in advance of first-hand experience in schools.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 4(03 Aug - 23 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Module 2: Chemical Sciences

Understanding the composition and behaviour of matter begins at the macro level using the 5 senses. The chemical and physical properties of substances are determined by their structure at an atomic scale: substances change, and new substances are produced by rearranging atoms through atomic interactions and energy transfer.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 2: Chemical Sciences Understanding the composition and behaviour of matter begins at the macro level using the 5 senses. The chemical and physical properties of substances are determined by their structure at an atomic scale: substances change, and new substances are produced by rearranging atoms through atomic interactions and energy transfer.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 5 To Week 6(24 Aug - 06 Sep)
learning_activities.topic:
Module 3: Physical Sciences

Understanding the nature of forces and motion, as well as matter and energy, goes beyond biologically primary knowledge required to survive and into the more abstract biologically secondary knowledge where we can visualise the invisible and build models, theories, and rules. Children experience push and pull, energy ups and downs and differences in the properties of materials, but only at the macroscopic level. To understand the abstract, they need hands-on examples of force, motion and energy transfer in their world.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 3: Physical Sciences Understanding the nature of forces and motion, as well as matter and energy, goes beyond biologically primary knowledge required to survive and into the more abstract biologically secondary knowledge where we can visualise the invisible and build models, theories, and rules. Children experience push and pull, energy ups and downs and differences in the properties of materials, but only at the macroscopic level. To understand the abstract, they need hands-on examples of force, motion and energy transfer in their world.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 7 To Week 8(07 Sep - 20 Sep)
learning_activities.topic:
Module 4: Biological Sciences

This module is about understanding living things. A diverse range of living things have evolved on Earth over hundreds of millions of years. Living things are interdependent and interact with each other and their environment. The form and features of living things are related to their body systems' functions.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 4: Biological Sciences This module is about understanding living things. A diverse range of living things have evolved on Earth over hundreds of millions of years. Living things are interdependent and interact with each other and their environment. The form and features of living things are related to their body systems' functions.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 9 To Week 13(21 Sep - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Module 5: Earth and Space Sciences

The Science Understanding: Earth and Space Sciences tends to bounce between space and our planet, so we will take a week to look at space and include some early human endeavours to make sense of the sky and beyond. Following the mid-semester break and placement weeks, we dive into the second week, which brings us back to Earth to explore sudden and long-term changes that affect our land and oceans. This module aligns with the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Curriculum resources that follow three themes: Astronomy, Water and Fire. The final week of the course is given for presentation of mini-lessons (Assessment task 4A).


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
